paper_analysis.evidence_text: The CVC-RF was implemented using the PyTorch framework and trained for 100 epochs on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 GPU, using AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-4. The EMA momentum coefficient α, CMCL temperature Coefficient τ , and loss Weight λ are set to 0.01, 0.01, and 0.2, respectively.
paper_analysis.notes: The paper provides detailed hyperparameters including learning rate, optimizer, and specific coefficients used in the model. This satisfies the criterion for reporting hyperparameters.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: All hyperparameters are determined through experiments on the validation set.
Table . 1 presents a comparison of CVC-RF with single-view methods, multi-view feature fusionand weighting approaches, as well as the results of ablation experiments for each module.
paper_analysis.notes: The paper provides performance comparisons with other methods but lacks detailed implementation and tuning information for these baselines, which is necessary for fair comparison.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: In the ablation study, we found that the removal of the DSAM or MoE module led to a performance drop, demonstrating their effectiveness. Moreover, ablating CMCL resulted in a significant 5.54% drop in Acc.
paper_analysis.notes: The paper includes an ablation study showing the impact of removing key components (DSAM, MoE, CMCL) on performance, which satisfies the sensitivity analysis criterion.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: 1,657 pairs of carotid US images (518 RADS1, 772 RADS2, 367 RADS3-4) from 1,228 patients, with each patient contributing no more than two cases.
paper_analysis.notes: The paper provides dataset size, class distribution, and patient contribution details, but lacks specific patient demographics and imaging parameters.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: All images were resized to 224×224 and randomly split into 7:1:2 for training, validation, and testing.
We collected a private dataset comprising Quantitative and Qualitative Analysis.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: This study was approved by the local IRB.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: The paper reports performance metrics such as accuracy, mean precision, mean recall, and mean F1-score, but does not mention standard deviation or confidence intervals.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: All results were evaluated using paired t-tests with p-values<0.05.
We evaluate the performance of CVC-RF using accuracy (Acc), mean precision (M-Pre), mean recall (M-Rec), mean F1-score (M-F1), and per-class accuracy (Acc1-3).
paper_analysis.notes: The paper lists the metrics used but does not provide definitions or formulas for them. This is important for reproducibility and understanding the evaluation.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: The model infers in 41.96 ms/case, with 18.73M parameters, 16.50 GFLOPs, demonstrating strong deployment potential.
The CVC-RF was implemented using the PyTorch framework and trained for 100 epochs on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 GPU, using AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-4.
paper_analysis.notes: The paper mentions the GPU used for training but does not specify the memory requirements or footprint.
The provided sections do not discuss failure cases or limitations of the method. The focus is on performance improvements and advantages over other methods.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: Accurate carotid plaque grading (CPG) is crucial for early diagnosis and prevention of stroke. Ultrasound (US) as a wide availability, realtime imaging modality, has become the primary technique used in CPG.
paper_analysis.notes: The paper discusses the clinical relevance of accurate carotid plaque grading for stroke prevention, highlighting the importance of the method in a clinical context.

paper_analysis.evidence_text: The CVC-RF was implemented using the PyTorch framework and trained for 100 epochs on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 GPU, using AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 1e-4.
